# Data Quality Report — Part 1

**Snapshot date:** 2025-09-30  |  **Customers in scope:** 2,400  |  Auto-generated by `eda_audit.ipynb`.

Before trusting any analysis, I went through every file looking for the kinds of problems that quietly break
downstream work — duplicates, gaps, impossible values, broken joins, and anything that could leak the future
into a model. Here is what I found and what I'd do about it.

## 1. Row counts line up with the data dictionary

| File | Rows | Cols |
|---|---|---|
| customers | 2,400 | 9 |
| orders | 10,009 | 10 |
| support_tickets | 1,921 | 8 |
| web_events_snapshot | 2,400 | 10 |
| churn_labels | 2,400 | 4 |
| rfm_modeling_snapshot | 2,400 | 29 |
| intervention_history | 2,400 | 5 |

Everything matches what the dictionary promised, so nothing was dropped or truncated on load.

## 2. Issues I found, and how I'd treat each

| Issue | Where | How much | What I'd do |
|---|---|---|---|
| Duplicate-like orders | `orders.order_id` (`*_DUP`) | 12 rows, identical content | Drop the `_DUP` rows before aggregating anything order-level |
| Missing `loyalty_tier` | `customers` | 1,386 (58%) | Null here means "never enrolled" — keep it as its own category rather than guessing |
| Missing `skin_type` | `customers` | 401 (17%) | Fill with "Unknown"; it carries little signal anyway |
| Missing `rating` | `orders` | 80 | Leave out of rating averages — don't fill with 0, which would fake a bad score |
| Outlier `gross_amount` | `orders` | 5 rows above INR 10k (max INR 24,789) | Cap at the 99th percentile (INR 2,309) when building spend features |
| Post-snapshot orders | `orders.order_date` | 1,872 rows | These exist to build the label — never use them as features |

## 3. Joins and keys

Every `customer_id` in orders, tickets, web events, churn labels and interventions points back to a real
customer — no orphan rows. All 2,400 customers have at least one pre-snapshot order, but only
1,247 of them (52%) ever raised a support
ticket. That's why every join has to be a left join from `customers`: a customer with no ticket is normal,
not an error.

## 4. Dates make sense

- signup_date: 2024-01-01 -> 2025-09-15
- order_date: 2024-01-09 -> 2025-11-29 (runs past the snapshot on purpose)
- ticket_date: 2024-01-13 -> 2025-09-30 (never crosses the snapshot)
- Orders placed before the customer even signed up: 0 — so the timeline is clean.

## 5. The leakage trap (most important)

The single biggest risk here is using the future to predict the future. Two things must stay out of any
feature set: the 1,872 order rows dated after 2025-09-30, and the `churn_next_60d` label itself.
The safest base to model on is `rfm_modeling_snapshot.csv`, which the dictionary already certifies as
leakage-free.
